planting.note: Take hardwood cuttings 8–12 inches long in late winter before bud break and insert directly into moist soil at the water's edge in full sun; rooting is rapid and reliable. Coastal Plain Willow establishes quickly from cuttings and can also be established by planting dormant rooted stems directly in saturated soil.

directions: Coastal Plain Willow is the most abundant native willow of the coastal plain wetlands, stream banks, tidal margins, and disturbed wet soils from Virginia south through Florida and west to Texas and the Caribbean, forming dense multi-stemmed thickets that provide critical cover and nesting habitat for waterfowl, shorebirds, and muskrats. Like all willows, it produces copious pollen and nectar in early spring that are among the first resources available to emerging queen bumblebees, native sweat bees, and early butterflies; the foliage hosts the caterpillars of Viceroy, Mourning Cloak, and numerous native moths. Propagate easily from hardwood cuttings taken in late winter before bud break, inserting 8–12 inch stems into moist, wet soil in full sun; rooting is rapid, establishment reliable, and fallen branches that contact moist soil root naturally. Coastal Plain Willow spreads aggressively by root suckers in wet areas and is best used in large, naturalized riparian settings where its vigorous growth habit is an asset.
sources: Native Trees of the Southeast (Kirkman, Brown, Leopold)

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and plant divisions in shallow water (0–6 inches deep) or at the water's edge in full sun to partial shade. American Water-Willow spreads by rhizomes to form dense colonies and is best established from divisions of existing clumps in wet, acidic, sandy or mucky streamside soil.

directions: American Water-Willow is a native aquatic and emergent perennial of clean streams, river margins, and shallow lake edges across eastern North America, where its rhizomatous colonies form dense mats of willow-like leaves in the current, catching and filtering silt while stabilizing stream banks. The bicolored white and violet flowers are produced prolifically from late spring through summer and are outstanding nectar sources for native bumblebees, hummingbirds, and sphinx moths visiting the shallow water's edge. Plant divisions in spring in shallow water (0–6 inches) or at the water's edge in full sun to partial shade; American Water-Willow spreads readily by rhizomes to form dense, erosion-controlling colonies and is an exceptional native plant for water garden edges, stream bank stabilization, and rain garden overflow zones where periodic flooding occurs. The dense underwater stems also provide important spawning and refuge habitat for small fish.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in wet, acidic, mucky to sandy, humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade near a water feature or in a rain garden; Alligator Lily requires consistently wet to waterlogged soil and will not tolerate drought. Divide bulb clumps every 3–4 years to maintain vigor.

directions: Alligator Lily is a Florida endemic spider-lily found in wet flatwoods, freshwater marshes, and wet prairie margins of south and central Florida, producing elegant white flowers with reflexed petals and a distinctive fringed central cup (corona) characteristic of the Hymenocallis genus. Rare in cultivation despite being well suited to wet gardens, pond margins, and water features, Alligator Lily is a true South Florida native restricted to the peninsula and not found naturally anywhere else in the world. Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in wet, acidic, mucky to sandy, humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade near a water feature or rain garden; established clumps multiply steadily through bulb offsets to form impressive colonies in appropriate wet conditions. Alligator Lily is intolerant of drought and requires consistently moist to wet soil — it performs best in natural pond margins, wet flatwood restoration plantings, and South Florida water gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to rocky coastal soil in full sun in Zone 10–11 frost-free landscapes. Mangrove Spider Lily tolerates salt spray and brief flooding once established. Divide overcrowded clumps every 3–4 years in fall to maintain vigor and flowering.

directions: Mangrove Spider Lily is a native bulbous perennial of southern Florida's coastal strand, coastal hammocks, and pine rocklands, as well as much of the Caribbean, producing spectacular, intensely fragrant white flowers with a fringed central cup and reflexed white petals on 18–30 inch stems from late spring through summer. The broad, strap-like evergreen leaves and showstopper flowers make it one of the most spectacular native perennials available for frost-free South Florida landscapes, and its tolerance of salt spray, periodic flooding, and moderate drought makes it practical for a wide range of coastal garden conditions. Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to rocky coastal soil in full sun; established clumps slowly expand through bulb offsets and become increasingly floriferous as they mature. Mangrove Spider Lily is cold-sensitive and is best suited to Zone 10–11, though it may survive brief cold snaps in protected Zone 9b microclimates.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist to moderately well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. Northern Prickly Ash spreads by root suckers and is useful for naturalized thickets and hedgerows; handle with care due to the sharp spines at branch nodes.

directions: Northern Prickly Ash is a native spiny shrub of open woods, thickets, and stream banks across eastern North America from Quebec south to the Gulf states, bearing paired spines at the branch nodes and aromatic compound leaves that release a sharp citrus fragrance when crushed. A member of the citrus family (Rutaceae) and close relative of the southern Hercules' Club, the aromatic bark contains numbing, anesthetic compounds that were applied by many Native American peoples — including the Ojibwe, Chippewa, and Cherokee — directly to sore gums for toothache relief; the bark was also used as a fish poison and to treat digestive ailments, earning the plant the folk name toothache tree. An important host plant for the giant swallowtail butterfly in the northern portion of its range, where it often replaces the closely related Zanthoxylum clava-herculis as the primary larval host plant, Northern Prickly Ash provides dense, spiny nesting cover for birds. Plant in moist to moderately well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; it spreads by root suckers to form thickets and is most useful in naturalized hedgerows and wildlife habitat plantings.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; provide a sturdy support or allow to scramble into a tree. Laurel Greenbriar is difficult to transplant from the wild due to its deep, tuberous root — use only container-grown nursery stock.

directions: Laurel Greenbriar is an evergreen native vine of moist woods, swamp margins, and thickets of the coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and west to Texas, climbing by tendrils to 30 feet or more with stiff, spiny stems and long, narrow, leathery evergreen leaves that make it the greenest plant in the winter understory forest. The small, dark blue-black berries are produced in clusters and are a critical winter food for wild turkeys, black bears, white-tailed deer, mockingbirds, and cedar waxwings when other food sources are exhausted. The spiny, interlocking stems create impenetrable thickets that provide ideal nesting and escape cover for ground-nesting birds, rabbits, and other small wildlife, and the tubers were historically eaten by Native Americans. Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Laurel Greenbriar tolerates periodic flooding and deep shade once established, and requires a sturdy structure or tree to climb.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Carolina Holly is dioecious — plant one male for every 2–3 females within 50 feet for reliable berry production. Seeds require double stratification and are slow to germinate.

directions: Carolina Holly is a native deciduous holly of the coastal plain and lower piedmont from the Carolinas south to Florida and west to Texas, growing as a large shrub or small tree at forest edges, hammock borders, and along stream banks in partial shade to full sun. The small but vivid red to orange-red berries, held in clusters along the bare winter branches after leaf drop, are an important food source for robins, cedar waxwings, bluebirds, hermit thrushes, and other fruit-eating birds during fall migration and winter. Like all native hollies, Carolina Holly is dioecious — both male and female plants are required within pollinator range for berry production — and its modest size compared to American Holly makes it suitable for smaller gardens where the larger species may overwhelm the space. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants tolerate moderate drought and require little maintenance.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in pure, infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun. Runner Oak requires xeric scrub conditions and will not compete in heavier or more productive soils; the rhizomatous root system means it spreads vigorously once established.

directions: Runner Oak is a low, colony-forming native shrub oak of the coastal plain sandhills and fire-maintained xeric scrub habitats of Georgia and Florida, spreading underground by rhizomes to form dense colonies of knee-high stems across expanses of pure white sand where larger trees cannot survive — making it superficially resemble a grass colony more than a tree. Despite its small stature, Runner Oak produces full-sized acorns that are a critical food source for Florida Scrub-Jays, gopher tortoises, and black bears in the imperiled scrub ecosystem where larger mast-producing oaks may be sparse. Highly adapted to fire, Runner Oak resprouts vigorously from its extensive underground rhizome system within weeks of a scrub burn and is a dominant component of the ground-layer plant community in many Florida scrub sites. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in infertile, pure, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun — Runner Oak requires the xeric, open, fire-maintained conditions of native scrub and will not thrive in more productive or shaded environments.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun. Bluejack Oak demands xeric, nutrient-poor conditions and will not tolerate clay, excessive moisture, or shading from taller vegetation.

directions: Bluejack Oak is a distinctive native tree of sandhills, xeric upland forest, and fire-maintained scrub across the southeastern coastal plain from Virginia to Texas, immediately recognizable among oaks by its blue-gray, paddle-shaped leaves with pale, hairy undersides that give the small tree a frosted, silvery appearance unlike any other eastern oak. Adapted to the driest, most nutrient-poor sandy soils of the coastal plain, Bluejack Oak is extremely drought-tolerant and fire-resilient, regenerating rapidly from its extensive root system after low-intensity fires that historically maintained the sandhills where it is one of the dominant trees. The small acorns ripen in fall and are a critical food source for white-tailed deer, wild turkeys, small mammals, and Florida Scrub-Jays in dry upland communities where larger mast-producing oaks may be absent. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in infertile, sandy, extremely well-drained, acidic soil in full sun — Bluejack Oak will not tolerate clay, excess moisture, or shade.
sources: Silvics of North America (USDA Forest Service)

planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall at 1–2 inch depth in well-drained, moderately fertile, acidic to neutral, loamy to clay-loam soil in full sun to light shade. Shingle Oak is more adaptable to clay and moderately compacted urban soils than many oaks and makes an excellent large shade tree for challenging sites.

directions: Shingle Oak is a distinctive and underappreciated native oak of the eastern United States from New England south to Georgia and west to Kansas, immediately recognizable among oaks by its entire, unlobed leaves resembling those of a water beech — a surprising feature for an oak — making it an excellent conversation piece in any landscape. The dried leaves persist on the branches well into winter in a quality called marcescence, providing landscape texture and winter interest, while the abundant small acorns are a reliable annual mast crop for wood ducks, turkeys, white-tailed deer, and numerous songbirds. An adaptable and durable large shade tree for urban and suburban landscapes, Shingle Oak tolerates heavier clay soils and greater pH variability than many oaks, and develops a graceful, broadly pyramidal to rounded crown at maturity with modest fall color ranging from yellow to russet brown. Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade.
sources: Silvics of North America (USDA Forest Service)
